   * mutated. The listener will receive a list of nodes along with the type of mutation
   * that was performed on each: created, destroyed, or updated.
   *
   * One common use case for this is to attach DOM event listeners to the underlying DOM nodes as Lexical nodes are created.
   * {@link LexicalEditor.getElementByKey} can be used for this.
   *
   * If any existing nodes are in the DOM, and skipInitialization is not true, the listener
   * will be called immediately with an updateTag of 'registerMutationListener' where all
   * nodes have the 'created' NodeMutation. This can be controlled with the skipInitialization option
   * (whose default was previously true for backwards compatibility with &lt;=0.16.1 but has been changed to false as of 0.21.0).
   *
   * @param klass - The class of the node that you want to listen to mutations on.
   * @param listener - The logic you want to run when the node is mutated.
   * @param options - see {@link MutationListenerOptions}
   * @returns a teardown function that can be used to cleanup the listener.
   */
  registerMutationListener(klass, listener, options) {
    const klassToMutate = this.resolveRegisteredNodeAfterReplacements(this.getRegisteredNode(klass)).klass;
    const mutations = this._listeners.mutation;
    let klassSet = mutations.get(listener);
    if (klassSet === undefined) {
      klassSet = new Set();
      mutations.set(listener, klassSet);
    }
    klassSet.add(klassToMutate);
    const skipInitialization = options && options.skipInitialization;
    if (!(skipInitialization === undefined ? DEFAULT_SKIP_INITIALIZATION : skipInitialization)) {
      this.initializeMutationListener(listener, klassToMutate);
    }
    return () => {
      klassSet.delete(klassToMutate);
      if (klassSet.size === 0) {
        mutations.delete(listener);
      }
    };
  }

  /** @internal */
  getRegisteredNode(klass) {
    const registeredNode = this._nodes.get(klass.getType());
    if (registeredNode === undefined) {
      {
        formatDevErrorMessage(`Node ${klass.name} has not been registered. Ensure node has been passed to createEditor.`);
      }
    }
    return registeredNode;
  }

  /** @internal */
  resolveRegisteredNodeAfterReplacements(registeredNode) {
    while (registeredNode.replaceWithKlass) {
      registeredNode = this.getRegisteredNode(registeredNode.replaceWithKlass);
    }
    return registeredNode;
  }

  /** @internal */
  initializeMutationListener(listener, klass) {
    const prevEditorState = this._editorState;
    const nodeMap = getCachedTypeToNodeMap(prevEditorState).get(klass.getType());
    if (!nodeMap) {
      return;
    }
    const nodeMutationMap = new Map();
    for (const k of nodeMap.keys()) {
      nodeMutationMap.set(k, 'created');
    }
    if (nodeMutationMap.size > 0) {
      listener(nodeMutationMap, {
        dirtyLeaves: new Set(),
        prevEditorState,
        updateTags: new Set(['registerMutationListener'])
      });
    }
  }

  /** @internal */
  registerNodeTransformToKlass(klass, listener) {
    const registeredNode = this.getRegisteredNode(klass);
    registeredNode.transforms.add(listener);
    return registeredNode;
  }

  /**
   * Registers a listener that will run when a Lexical node of the provided class is
   * marked dirty during an update. The listener will continue to run as long as the node
   * is marked dirty. There are no guarantees around the order of transform execution!

   * Watch out for infinite loops. See [Node Transforms](https://lexical.dev/docs/concepts/transforms)

  registerNodeTransform(klass, listener) {
    const registeredNode = this.registerNodeTransformToKlass(klass, listener);

      registeredNodes.push(registeredReplaceWithNode);
    }
    markNodesWithTypesAsDirty(this, registeredNodes.map(node => node.klass.getType()));
    return () => {
      registeredNodes.forEach(node => node.transforms.delete(listener));
    };
  }

  hasNode(node) {
    return this._nodes.has(node.getType());
  }

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
**Summarizing code references and suggestions**

I’m putting together a clear summary of key code areas like editor config, runtime node replacement, and import/deserialization handling, linking to specific line ranges in `Lexical.dev.js` and `LexicalEditor.d.ts`. I’ll highlight how mis-registration can cause default decorator nodes to return null and offer practical next steps to verify node replacement and JSON import implementations, keeping the explanation accessible and structured with bullet points and a suggestions list.
</details></think>

_**Agent (gpt-5.1-codex-mini 2026-02-25 09:59:13Z)**_

Lexical 0.39’s `initialConfig.nodes` accepts either node constructors or replacement descriptors (`{ replace, with, withKlass }`). `createEditor` extracts those fields, validates that `withKlass` extends the base class, stores the `replace` callback and descendant key in `_nodes`, and builds shared node state for the original `replace` chain so runtime transforms and factory helpers know where to hand off (see `LexicalEditor.d.ts:131‑150` plus `Lexical.dev.js:10133‑10204`). Replacement is honored whenever nodes are created (factories call `$applyNodeReplacement` and validate the returned instance, `withKlass`, and key) and when `$create` or mutation listeners resolve a registered node: `resolveRegisteredNodeAfterReplacements` follows the `replaceWithKlass` chain so `$create(Replace)` actually instantiates the custom subclass (`Lexical.dev.js:10497‑10585`, `11996‑12014`, `12455‑12482`).

Serialization/deserialization still hinge on `_nodes` keyed by each node’s `getType()`. `parseEditorState` looks up `registeredNodes.get(serializedNode.type)` and calls that class’s `importJSON` to rebuild the instance (`Lexical.dev.js:8260‑8335`). If the replacement class isn’t registered under the serialized type (e.g., you forgot to include the custom decorator’s constructor or mis-specified `withKlass`), parsing either throws (“type not found”) or instantiates the base node, so your subclass’s state/`decorate` logic never runs. `DecoratorNode`’s default `decorate` returns `null`, and the reconciler only calls `.decorate(...)` when `$isDecoratorNode(r)` passes, so the missing custom instance means no DOM render for your decorator subtype (`Lexical.dev.js:9653‑9675`, `10880‑10930`).

**Registration risk**
A missing or incorrect replacement entry (e.g., not providing the replacement constructor in `nodes` or mis-matching `withKlass`) leaves the editor with only the base decorator type during deserialization, so `decorate()` still returns `null` and the custom decorator is never rendered. Always include the custom class, provide the `with` function that returns a fresh node with a new key, and make `withKlass` point to that class so `$applyNodeReplacement` and `parseEditorState` can reach it.

Suggestions:
1. Re-check your `initialConfig.nodes` list so each replacement descriptor lists the original node via `replace`, a factory via `with`, and the actual replacement class via `withKlass`, and also include the replacement class itself in the array; this satisfies the validations at `Lexical.dev.js:10155‑10204` and the type declared in `LexicalEditor.d.ts:131‑150`.
2. Confirm your replacement class implements `static importJSON` (or inherits the fallback set in `Lexical.dev.js:12440‑12447`) and is registered when parsing (`_nodes` lookup at `Lexical.dev.js:8260‑8335`), otherwise the deserializer falls back to a non-decorating base node and your `decorate()` never fires.

_**Agent (gpt-5.1-codex-mini 2026-02-25 10:02:42Z)**_

**Registration Insight**  
When the editor builds `_nodes`, each entry is keyed by the base class’s `getType()` and stores the class you passed in via `replace` along with its `replace`/`withKlass` metadata. That’s visible in `Lexical.dev.js` lines **10133‑10205**: the loop pulls `klass = options.replace` when an object is supplied, validates `replaceWithKlass`, and then writes `{ klass, replace, replaceWithKlass, … }` for `klass.getType()` into `registeredNodes`. So `footnote-section` is registered with `klass === FootnoteSectionNode` even if you only mention `FootnoteSectionEditNode` inside the replacement descriptor (because the descriptor isn’t registered as a standalone constructor).

**Deserialization Behavior**  
`parseEditorState` always rehydrates by looking up the serialized `type` in `_nodes` and calling that registered entry’s `klass.importJSON`. Lines **8287‑8310** show it never runs the `replace` or `with` hooks; it directly instantiates `registeredNode.klass` and appends its children. Therefore, if the replacement class was not also registered separately, parsing will rebuild plain `FootnoteSectionNode` instances — the replacement step happens only later when you explicitly call `$applyNodeReplacement` or otherwise run the `replace` callback, not as part of `parseEditorState`.

**Conclusion**  
With only `[FootnoteSectionNode, { replace: FootnoteSectionNode, … withKlass: FootnoteSectionEditNode }]` in `nodes`, Lexical registers `FootnoteSectionNode` for `'footnote-section'` and `parseEditorState` instantiates that base class directly (no automatic swap to `FootnoteSectionEditNode`).

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
**Clarifying $nodesOfType behavior with subclasses**

I’m focusing on how $nodesOfType checks nodes by both runtime class (using `instanceof`) and the node’s `__type` string to match the class’s type, explaining that when a subclass like FootnoteSectionEditNode extends FootnoteSectionNode and shares the same type string, the method includes those instances. I’ll reference Lexical.dev.js lines 11769-11783 to back this explanation.
</details></think>

_**Agent (gpt-5.1-codex-mini 2026-02-25 10:13:30Z)**_

`$nodesOfType(Klass)` reads `klass.getType()` once, then iterates every node in the active editor’s `_nodeMap`. For each node it only pushes it into the result list when all three conditions are true: the node is an `instanceof klass`, its stored `__type` equals `klassType`, and it’s still attached to the tree. That logic is exactly in `Lexical.dev.js` lines **11769‑11783** (see the loop in `$nodesOfType`).

Because a replacement subclass such as `FootnoteSectionEditNode` both extends `FootnoteSectionNode` and keeps the same static `getType()` string (the replacement configuration is keyed by `'footnote-section'`), the runtime node passes `instanceof FootnoteSectionNode` and has `__type === 'footnote-section'`. Therefore `$nodesOfType(FootnoteSectionNode)` does include instances whose runtime class is `FootnoteSectionEditNode`, provided they share the same type string and are registered/attached.
